county_metadata.record_losses: 1712 Tuscarora War destroyed early precinct records. New Bern courthouse fire 1774 destroyed some colonial court records. Despite losses, Craven retains extensive records from 1730s forward due to its role as colonial capital.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Originally Archdale Precinct (1705), renamed Craven Precinct 1712. New Bern served as colonial capital 1770-1792. Pre-1712 records in Bath County papers. Craven is parent to Carteret (1722), Jones (1779), Lenoir (1791), and indirectly to many eastern NC counties. Tryon Palace (governor's residence) records at NC State Archives.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch deeds and wills — exceptional colonial-era collections. The Craven County Genealogical Society is active. New Bern Historical Society has significant local collections. For colonial-era research, check Secretary of State land grants and Colonial Court Records at State Archives. County ROD has deeds from 1739.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: index and images — modern records online, historical records via office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Deeds from 1739 (colonial era) through 1955 with indexes. Among the earliest surviving deed records in eastern NC.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1739 deed records lost in Tuscarora War destruction.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Wills, inventories, and accounts from colonial era forward. Significant for tracing colonial families in the New Bern area.
Court of Pleas and Quarter Sessions minutes from 1730. Colonial-era court records are among the earliest surviving in NC. Also includes Superior Court records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some gaps from 1774 courthouse fire, but most colonial court records survived.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage bonds from 1741 and marriage registers from 1851. Colonial-era bonds are particularly valuable for eastern NC genealogy.

source_profiles.source_name: Freedmen's Bureau Records — New Bern Sub-District
source_profiles.coverage_notes: New Bern was a major Freedmen's Bureau office. Records include labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, and ration records. Critical for African American genealogy in eastern NC.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1831 Fayetteville fire destroyed 600+ buildings including ALL church records except Methodist. Government records survived. Sherman destroyed Confederate Arsenal and Fayetteville Observer offices March 1865. No known courthouse record losses.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Bladen 1754. Pre-1754 families must be searched in Bladen. Cumberland and Sampson fell under Fayetteville District Superior Court (SR.398). Fort Bragg (est. 1918, renamed Fort Liberty 2023, re-renamed Fort Bragg 2025) — federal military records at NARA.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cumberland has one of the most complete continuous records in the region — deeds from 1752 (pre-formation!), court records from 1755, probate from 1757. The ROD website is exceptional: ALL deed books from Book 1 (1754) fully digitized and free online. For pre-1831 church records, only First Presbyterian (1792+) and Hay Street Methodist (1809+) survive on FamilySearch. Cumberland County Genealogical Society dissolved Dec 2023 but publications survive on FamilySearch Digital Library. All 8 county library branches are FamilySearch Affiliate Libraries.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: images and indexes — all land records from Book 1 (1754) to present, fully digitized

source_profiles.source_name: Cumberland County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: ALL land record index books 1754-1976 scanned. ALL land record images from Book 1 (1754) to present available online. Continuous, fully digitized. One of the best ROD portals in North Carolina.
source_profiles.survival_notes: No known losses. Pre-formation deeds from 1752 also included.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catalog #328786. Digitized microfilm. Redundant with ROD portal but accessible via FamilySearch API.

source_profiles.source_name: Cumberland County Wills and Estate Papers 1663-1978
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catalog #456876. Browse-only images (not name-indexed). Will Books A-P (1766-1962). Estate records extensive, arranged alphabetically.
Tax lists for 1755, 1767, 1777-1793, 1815, 1816-1823. The 1755 list is one of the earliest surviving tax records for any county in the region.
Microfilm. Most important surviving early church record for Cumberland. Survived the 1831 fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1831 fire destroyed all other Fayetteville church records except Methodist.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catalog #329016. Marriage bonds from 1803 and marriage registers from 1851. Index available.
One of the longest-running newspapers in NC. Digitized issues available. Sherman's troops destroyed the presses in 1865.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Post-1865 issues restart with new equipment.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1842 destroyed most records. Earlier 1803 fire damaged some records. Deeds survive from 1696 only because they were recopied from memory and adjacent county records. Marriage bonds survive only from 1851. Court minutes fragmented before 1799.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the original Albemarle precincts (1668), became a county 1739. Pre-1668 records in Albemarle County papers at NC State Archives. Currituck once extended into what is now Virginia — boundary disputes mean some families appear in both NC and VA records. Outer Banks communities (Corolla, Duck) were part of Currituck.
county_metadata.research_tips: Start with FamilySearch deeds (1696+) — these are the recopied records and represent a remarkable salvage effort. For colonial-era, check VA colonial records as well due to boundary disputes. Currituck County Historical Society has local publications. Census records from 1790 forward are complete. The Outer Banks History Center in Manteo has relevant materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: limited — office contact info, no remote image access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Deeds from 1696 (recopied after 1842 fire) through 1944. Among the oldest surviving land records in NC, predating most county formations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Original deeds destroyed in 1842 fire; these are reconstructions from memory and neighboring county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Wills, inventories, and estate accounts. Earliest wills from 1713 are among the oldest probate records in NC.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some gaps due to courthouse fires, but remarkable survival rate for such early records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage registers from 1851 forward. All pre-1842 marriage bonds destroyed in courthouse fire.
source_profiles.survival_notes: No marriage records survive before 1851 due to courthouse fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court of Pleas and Quarter Sessions minutes, Superior Court records. Pre-1799 court minutes largely lost to fires.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known courthouse record losses. However, as a very young county (1870), some early community records were informal.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1870 from parts of Currituck, Tyrrell, and Hyde counties. The youngest eastern NC county. Pre-1870 records scattered across three parent counties. Roanoke Island (Lost Colony site, Civil War Battle of Roanoke Island 1862) and the Outer Banks are in Dare. Hatteras village records may appear in Hyde County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pre-1870 research requires checking Currituck, Tyrrell, and Hyde county records depending on location. The Outer Banks History Center in Manteo is the premier local research facility. FamilySearch has deeds from 1870. Census records from 1870 forward. For Outer Banks families, maritime and lifesaving station records at NARA are valuable.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: limited — office info, no remote image access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized microfilm. Complete deed records from county formation. Indexes included.
source_profiles.survival_notes: No losses — young county with intact records.

source_profiles.source_name: Dare County Wills and Estate Records 1870-1960
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, and estate accounts from county formation.
Marriage registers from county formation. Complete.

source_profiles.source_name: US Life-Saving Service and Coast Guard Station Records — Outer Banks
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Life-Saving Service station logs (1874+) and Coast Guard records document Outer Banks families. Station keepers and surfmen are named with personal details. Unique source for barrier island families.
